pack_name: Senior Java — Customer Zero Seed (10 Qs)

smoke_test_subset.description: MCQ-only subset for Customer Zero Day-1 first run

metadata.authored_by: QOrium AI v0.5 + CTO Office Authoring Loop

questions.title: Java Virtual Machine Memory Model Fundamentals
questions.citation: Java Language Specification Ch.17 (Threads and Locks); JVM Specification §2.5
questions.body: In Java's memory model, what is the primary purpose of the happens-before relationship defined in the Java Memory Model (JMM)?

options.text: To ensure that memory writes are physically ordered by CPU cache coherence
To provide a guarantee that one operation's effects are visible to a subsequent operation, even across threads
To prevent the JVM from applying any optimizations to multithreaded code
To enforce that all threads use the same CPU core for execution

questions.answer_explanation: Happens-before guarantees if action A happens-before action B, then A's memory effects are visible to B. Stronger than physical ordering, weaker than serialization. Allows JVM optimizations while preserving correctness for properly synchronized code. References: JLS §17.4 Memory Model.

questions.title: Spring Boot Dependency Injection & Bean Lifecycle
questions.citation: Spring Boot 3.0+ Reference Documentation §7 (Beans and Dependency Injection)
questions.body: In Spring Boot 3.x, when using `@Autowired` on a constructor parameter, what is the intended behavior if the bean is not found and the field is not marked as `required = false`?

options.text: Spring ignores the missing bean and leaves the field null
Spring throws a `NoSuchBeanDefinitionException` at application startup (before the context is fully initialized)
Spring defers the lookup until the bean is first accessed at runtime
Spring falls back to creating a new instance of the required type using the default constructor

questions.answer_explanation: @Autowired with required=true (default) fails fast on startup with NoSuchBeanDefinitionException if the bean cannot be resolved. References: Spring Framework 6.x §7.9.3.

questions.title: Virtual Threads & Project Loom
questions.citation: JEP 444: Virtual Threads (Java 21); Project Loom design notes
questions.body: Java 21 introduces virtual threads via Project Loom. Which statement most accurately captures their value proposition?

options.text: Virtual threads eliminate the need for asynchronous programming (e.g., CompletableFuture or reactive frameworks)
Virtual threads allow millions of concurrent tasks with minimal memory overhead, enabling simpler synchronous-style code that scales
Virtual threads guarantee faster execution than platform threads because they bypass the OS kernel
Virtual threads automatically parallelize single-threaded code across multiple CPU cores

questions.answer_explanation: Virtual threads are lightweight (~1KB stack base), letting JVM schedule millions on a small carrier-thread pool. Synchronous code regains scalability. They do NOT replace async APIs and do NOT guarantee throughput.

questions.title: JPA/Hibernate Lazy Loading & Session Management
questions.citation: JPA 3.0 Specification §3.2; Hibernate 6.x User Guide §11 (Sessions and Transactions)
questions.body: A Customer entity has a lazily-loaded @OneToMany relationship to Orders. The service queries the customer inside a @Transactional method, returns it, and the controller serializes it to JSON outside the transaction boundary. What happens?

options.text: `LazyInitializationException` — the Hibernate session is closed, and the proxy cannot be initialized
`NullPointerException` — lazy-loaded collections are null until explicitly joined
`TransactionRequiredException` — accessing lazy relationships requires an active transaction
`NoSuchElementException` — Hibernate cannot locate the associated Orders rows

questions.answer_explanation: Classic LazyInitializationException — proxy de-references after session close. Fix: Open Session In View, fetch join, or DTO projection inside the transaction.

questions.title: Spring Transactional Propagation Levels
questions.citation: Spring Framework Reference §16 (Transaction Management); @Transactional propagation modes
questions.body: Method `processOrder()` uses `@Transactional(propagation = REQUIRED)`. Inside, it calls `logAudit()` which uses `@Transactional(propagation = NESTED)`. `logAudit()` throws a `RuntimeException`. What is the outcome?

options.text: Both `processOrder()` and `logAudit()` transactions are rolled back
The `logAudit()` savepoint is rolled back, but `processOrder()` can continue if the exception is caught
`logAudit()` commits independently; `processOrder()` continues unaffected
A `TransactionException` is thrown because NESTED is not compatible with REQUIRES_NEW

questions.answer_explanation: NESTED creates a savepoint within the outer transaction. The inner can be rolled back without aborting the outer — provided the exception is caught at the call site. Requires JDBC 3.0+ Savepoint support.

questions.title: Microservices Resilience Pattern — Circuit Breaker
questions.citation: Resilience4j 2.x docs; Microservices Patterns (Chris Richardson) Ch.5
questions.body: A circuit breaker is in OPEN state after repeated upstream failures. By default, when does it transition out of OPEN?

options.text: Automatically after a fixed time period (wait duration) elapses; transitions to HALF_OPEN to test recovery
Only when the upstream service explicitly signals recovery (via a callback)
Immediately upon the next successful request
When an administrator manually resets the circuit breaker

questions.answer_explanation: Standard pattern: OPEN→HALF_OPEN after wait duration; in HALF_OPEN, a limited number of trial requests determine CLOSED (recovered) vs back-to-OPEN.

questions.title: Bulk Insert with Idempotency & Transactional Retry
questions.citation: JDBC 4.3 batch updates; Spring Boot @Retryable + @Transactional patterns
questions.body: Implement a `bulkInsertOrders(List<Order> orders)` method that: (1) is idempotent on `external_order_id`; (2) uses JDBC batch with `rewriteBatchedStatements=true`; (3) retries on transient SQLException up to 3 times with exponential backoff; (4) is transactional for atomicity per batch but NOT for the entire list (chunked at 1000). Provide service code + test plan.
questions.answer_explanation: 5-point rubric. Required elements: (1) `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (external_order_id) DO NOTHING` or equivalent UPSERT; (2) `PreparedStatement.addBatch()` + `executeBatch()` with chunk size 1000; (3) @Retryable with backoff(multiplier=2, maxAttempts=3); (4) @Transactional at the chunk level not the list level; (5) test plan covers idempotent re-run, partial-failure rollback, transient-error retry exhaustion, and chunk boundary correctness.

criteria.name: idempotency_on_external_id
criteria.must_have: ON CONFLICT or merge or insert-where-not-exists

criteria.name: transaction_scope_correct
criteria.must_have: transaction at chunk not at list level

criteria.name: test_plan_quality
criteria.must_have: covers idempotent rerun + partial-failure + retry exhaustion

questions.title: Virtual Thread-Safe Rate Limiter Design
questions.citation: Java 21 Virtual Threads (JEP 444); java.util.concurrent locks; token-bucket algorithm
questions.body: Design and implement a token-bucket rate limiter that is correct under millions of concurrent virtual-thread callers. Constraints: max 1,000 req/s sustained, 10 token burst, no starvation, no thread pinning. Provide the class + a 30-line stress-test sketch.
questions.answer_explanation: 5-point rubric. Must have: (1) AtomicLong / LongAdder for token count, NOT synchronized blocks (would pin virtual threads to carriers); (2) refill via System.nanoTime() delta computation, not a scheduler thread; (3) CAS loop on token decrement to avoid lock contention; (4) explicit Thread.onSpinWait() / parkNanos() backoff in the failure branch; (5) stress test starts millions of virtual threads, asserts steady-state ~1000 acquisitions/sec.

criteria.name: lock_free_design
criteria.must_have: no synchronized — uses CAS / AtomicLong / LongAdder

criteria.name: test_plan
criteria.must_have: millions of vthreads + observed throughput assertion

questions.title: Stream API Windowing Aggregation
questions.body: Given `Stream<Trade>` (millions of trades, each with `instant` and `notional`), compute: (a) total notional per 5-minute window; (b) the single highest-notional trade per window; in one stream pass; produce `Stream<WindowSummary>`.
questions.answer_explanation: 5-point rubric. Must have: (1) groupingBy(window-key derived from Instant truncated to 5-min bucket); (2) Collectors.teeing(summingDouble, maxBy) or compatible composite; (3) downstream collector building WindowSummary; (4) ordered output by window-start; (5) memory-bounded — does not collect all trades in memory.

criteria.name: windowing_correct
criteria.must_have: Instant truncated to 5-min bucket as group key

criteria.name: memory_bounded
criteria.must_have: documented memory profile or use of Spliterator/parallel correctness

questions.title: Distributed Transaction Choreography vs Saga Pattern
questions.citation: Microservices Patterns (Chris Richardson) Ch.4 (Saga); Eventuate / Axon Framework
questions.body: Design event-driven choreography for a 4-service assessment-delivery flow (Assessment, Question, Scoring, Notification). Address: saga pattern, compensating transactions, idempotency, exactly-once semantics, choreography vs orchestration trade-offs.
questions.answer_explanation: 5-point rubric. Choreography over orchestration (justified) · per-service event flow + compensation events · idempotency keys (event_id UUIDs) · inbox pattern for exactly-once · failure → emit Rollback event · trade-off acknowledgment (choreography harder to debug, more resilient).

criteria.name: saga_pattern_correctness
criteria.must_have: compensating events for each step + rollback path

criteria.name: operational_observability
criteria.must_have: saga state visualization plan / tracing plan
